content:
The **Jacobian** is named after **Carl Gustav Jacob Jacobi** (1804–1851), who developed determinants and functional matrices in the study of coordinate transformations and differential equations.

The **Hessian** honors **Ludwig Otto Hesse** (1811–1874), who used second-order derivative matrices to classify critical points of multivariate functions—the same min/max/saddle tests taught in calculus today.

In the 20th century, multivariable calculus merged with optimization: **Newton's method** in n dimensions relies on the Hessian inverse, and **KKT** theory uses second-order conditions for constrained optima.

Automatic differentiation (1970s onward) made Jacobians operational at scale. Modern deep learning rarely stores full Hessians but still depends on Jacobians implicitly in every `backward()` call, and revives second-order ideas selectively in quasi-Newton optimizers, sharpness-aware training, and normalizing-flow density evaluation.

timeline.label: 19th century
timeline.description: Jacobi and Hesse formalize first- and second-order derivative matrices for multivariate functions.

timeline.label: Newton in n dimensions
timeline.description: Hessian-based updates become the textbook second-order optimization method.

timeline.label: Automatic differentiation
timeline.description: Exact Jacobians computed from program structure—foundation of modern autograd.

timeline.label: Deep learning era
timeline.description: Backprop uses Jacobian chain products; Hessian eigenanalysis studies loss geometry without full H storage.
